words: This conversation with IDA MIELKE NEWMAN TOOK PLACE at her home at Cameron on
February 18,1977. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.

speaker: SS
words: When did your people first come here?

speaker: SS
words: IDA MIELKE NEWMAN: They came here in 1901. I was three years old.

speaker: SS
words: And where was it they came from?

speaker: IMN
words: Minnesota. Oh, there were quite a number of families came here from

speaker: IMN
words: Minnesota. Reverend Hopf was a minister- was their minister in Minnesota

speaker: IMN
words: and he moved to Cameron and he told congre

speaker: IMN
words: gation what a wonderful country it was. So I don't Inow how many

speaker: IMN
words: families- seven or eight families came through him. They were mem

speaker: IMN
words: bers of his congregation.

speaker: SS
words: Where in Minnesota?

speaker: IMN
words: From Arlington and New Auburn.

speaker: SS
words: Were your parents born in Germany?

speaker: IMN
words: My mother was born in Germany and my father was born in Minnesota.

speaker: IMN
words: My mother was four years old when her parents came across.

speaker: SS
words: So then your fa^fcer and mother both probably spoke English pretty

speaker: SS
words: well?

speaker: IMN
words: My dad did; my mother spoken broken English, because in Minnesota

speaker: IMN
words: where they lived they were all German, everyone talked German. And

speaker: IMN
words: even here when I was a youngster everyone talked German. I couldn't

speaker: IMN
words: talk English when I started to school. Are there other things you'd like to know?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, yes. I'm just getting started really.

speaker: SS
words: When they came, your parents, did they have much?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, no, people were very poor. They had I think, I think my dad

speaker: IMN
words: bought 160 acres, and I don't know just what he paid down whether it

speaker: IMN
words: was$1,000 or $2,000, and that's all they had. And, of course, they

speaker: IMN
words: brought along part of their goods.

speaker: IMN
words: Did they have much of an outfit to bring?

speaker: IMN
words: No, as far as machinery is concerned, no. Just their clothes and I

speaker: IMN
words: think they brought oh, some of their household goods.

speaker: IMN
words: Did they come with other families at the same time?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. My grandparents came at the same time, and I don't know just

speaker: IMN
words: what other families. And then some other families came the next

speaker: IMN
words: year, in 1902. The Hardungs and the Mielkes came in 1901 and the

speaker: IMN
words: Wagners and the Blicks and Bront - I was trying to think of some

speaker: IMN
words: more names- came the next year.

speaker: IMN
words: When you came were you old enough to remember that at all?

speaker: IMN
words: I remember very, very little. See I was only four- I wasn't even

speaker: IMN
words: four, I was only three. And I don t know I remember it or whether

speaker: IMN
words: I just remember from hearsay, you know, a great many times they'll

speaker: IMN
words: talk about it and you just think that you remember.

speaker: IMN
words: Right. Did they say it was rough at all? Or was it an easy- they

speaker: IMN
words: took the train out here.

speaker: IMN
words: They took the train and my mother always said when she came to Kendrick,

speaker: IMN
words: she says, "Oh, my goodness, how in the world are we ever going

speaker: IMN
words: to get out of this hole?" See, Minnesota everything was level, and

speaker: IMN
words: she didn't they'd ever get out of this. She said, "There can't

speaker: IMN
words: be any farming in country like this." (Laughter)

speaker: IMN
words: It probably was a disappointment then.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, she wasn't really disappointed, because she knew my dad wouldn't

speaker: IMN
words: buy something that he couldn't take care of.

speaker: IMN
words: Was he out here before she?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Yes, he was out here and bought the farm before she came out.

speaker: IMN
words: How many brothers and sisters?

speaker: IMN
words: Let's see, there was seven of us. There were three brothers born in

speaker: IMN
words: Minnesota and my sister and I were born in Minnesota and then two

speaker: IMN
words: brothers were born in Cameron. And the place that my father bought

speaker: IMN
words: was the old Cameron place, Cameron was named after. See, Mr.

speaker: IMN
words: Cameron had the first post office and he was quite a prominient man

speaker: IMN
words: and so when he was county commissioner at Lewiston-

speaker: SS
words: Was he German, too? Didn't sound like it.

speaker: IMN
words: No, he wasn't German.

speaker: SS
words: Did the community start off then not as a German community?

speaker: IMN
words: No, it didn't start as a German community.

speaker: SS
words: It became that way-

speaker: IMN
words: Tt became that way through the church, I think. The church was built

speaker: IMN
words: and through the church, the Lutheran Church, the people came and set

speaker: IMN
words: tled here. It was mostly theouth that that they settled right around

speaker: IMN
words: Cameron.

speaker: SS
words: Did the church draw on a big area, do you think, to start with?

speaker: IMN
words: No, just Cameron, just, Cameron, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was Hopf the first minister?

speaker: IMN
words: No. It's in the book who the first minister-

speaker: SS
words: I know Hattie Johnson told me the first minister was from Germany,

speaker: SS
words: he and his wife.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And she had a difficult time adapting, she said, to the country.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, I see.

speaker: SS
words: To get used to it. When you were old enough to really remembering

speaker: SS
words: things, what was it like around here when you were a kid?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, there were lots and lots of trees, but the contour of the coun

speaker: IMN
words: try is different because people had to make a living, you couldn't

speaker: IMN
words: go to the store and buy things, just, you know, your very necessities

speaker: IMN
words: And you have to have your own cattle, and your own fruit and your

speaker: IMN
words: garden. You raised your own things, your chickens and your pigs

speaker: IMN
words: that you butchered in the fall, and cured your meat and that was your

speaker: IMN
words: meat supply for the year, and your chickens. And you canned your

speaker: IMN
words: fruit and you made sauerkraut and you had your potatoes and

speaker: IMN
words: apples. So many apples that you stored in the winter. And made

speaker: IMN
words: your own butter.

speaker: SS
words: Was it densely settled then?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, much more so than now. The farms were, oh, forty acres, eighty

speaker: IMN
words: acres and 160 acres was a fairly good sized farm. And the people

speaker: IMN
words: farmed with horses; usually had four horses and a footburner and

speaker: IMN
words: seeder and a harrow. You know, footburner, just a hookon plow

speaker: IMN
words: and that's about all you had.

speaker: SS
words: How much neighboring? Was there a lot?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, there was a lot of neighboring. People visited back and

speaker: IMN
words: forth. You go over to some people's place on Sunday for dinner and

speaker: IMN
words: you eat what they had, and you were very welcome. And people visi

speaker: IMN
words: ted back and forth a lot. And in the evenings, a lot of times,

speaker: IMN
words: get together and play cards or tell stories. You know, stories

speaker: IMN
words: handed down fuorn generation to generation.

speaker: SS
words: Stories originating in the old country?

speaker: IMN
words: From the old country, yes. And then, of course, my parents tell

speaker: IMN
words: stories from Minnesota.

speaker: SS
words: When you say they were handed down; I don't expect you remember the

speaker: SS
words: stories themselves, but what kind of things would they be likely to

speaker: SS
words: be about?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, one thing that I remember- I don't remember the story itself,

speaker: IMN
words: but my grandmother, she was born in 1827, she lived with us because

speaker: IMN
words: grandfather had passed away, was telling about spooks. (Chuckles)

speaker: IMN
words: Of course, things we don't believe in now, but- she didn't be

speaker: IMN
words: lieve in it either but she was telling about it and oh, we thought

speaker: IMN
words: those stories were wonderful! And then they used to tell about the

speaker: IMN
words: different people, about different things that they did.

speaker: SS
words: You don't remember what she had to say about spooks-

speaker: IMN
words: No, I wish to goodness I could.

speaker: SS
words: I'll bet that would be fun.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. And when she was in Germany they were in Prussia, they were near

speaker: IMN
words: the Polish border, and I remember her teaching me some Polish and

speaker: IMN
words: oh, I wish I could remember it! I can remember-(Polish) one, two,

speaker: IMN
words: three more Polish. And I wish I could remember more.

speaker: SS
words: There was an awful lot of German spoken here at that time.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was most of the talking in German?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, the talking was all in German. And, oh, I don't really know

speaker: IMN
words: when they started to talk English, I suppose when the children had

speaker: IMN
words: to go to school. h After I was older we had dances, we had dances and

speaker: IMN
words: we'd have parties, and on Friday we'd go over to somebody's house

speaker: IMN
words: in the afternoon, young people'd meet, and we'd talk and

speaker: IMN
words: and we just had a lot of fun. And the celebrations when I was a

speaker: IMN
words: youngster were the Fourth of July- they'd meet at some- oh, in some

speaker: IMN
words: grove or in some people's pasture, we had nice green pastures and

speaker: IMN
words: play cards- not play cards- but play games, baseball and races.

speaker: SS
words: Did you play- were there outdoor games that you used to play like

speaker: SS
words: circle games and that kind of stuff?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, yeah, we used to play Drop-The-Handkerchief, then in the win

speaker: IMN
words: ter Fox-and-Goose, and I was trying to think of some other games.

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, there were different ' that we played, in addition to base

speaker: IMN
words: ball.

speaker: SS
words: You say dances? Dancing was quite acceptable, nobody objected to

speaker: SS
words: having dances around here?

speaker: SS
words: Some places I know they did.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. No, they didn't. Of course, there were certain people that

speaker: IMN
words: it was against their religion and so on didn't go to dances,

speaker: IMN
words: but most of 'em did. And it was of course, like in Cameron, when we

speaker: IMN
words: had the dances, it was just a whole group, of course,they had some

speaker: IMN
words: At that s about two miles away, that's about as far away we got.

speaker: SS
words: Dances at homes or in the school?

speaker: IMN
words: No, in the homes. At first we had 'em in the homes then after while

speaker: IMN
words: a hall at Cameron, used to be the old post office, we used

speaker: IMN
words: to have 'em in there.

speaker: SS
words: Be the whole neighborhood or just a few neighbors?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, it was in the neighborhood then a few of the young people around

speaker: IMN
words: And then another thing we had when I went to school was Literary Soc

speaker: IMN
words: ieties. And they were a lot of fun. Had debates and oh, recitations

speaker: IMN
words: and singing. And one of the things that was a lot of fun- they had

speaker: IMN
words: a paper called the "Hog's Eye" and it would be some stories made up

speaker: IMN
words: of people in the neighborhood. We always thought that was a lot of

speaker: IMN
words: fun.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder why the "Hog's Eye"? That's kind of an amusing name for it.

speaker: IMN
words: It was supposed to he amusing, it was for fun.

speaker: SS
words: Joke.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Would the stories be true, or would they be—

speaker: IMN
words: No, no, they wouldn't be true. No, they were just for fun.

speaker: SS
words: When you had say, like debates, would that be taking two sides of a

speaker: SS
words: question?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Would,they be serious?

speaker: IMN
words: They were real serious debates. I remember one of the jokes that

speaker: IMN
words: the "Hog's Eye"- one of the menat the table- someone asked one of

speaker: IMN
words: the children in school, "What does your father do when you sit down

speaker: IMN
words: to eat before you take your food?" "Well," he says, "my father says,

speaker: IMN
words: "go easy with the butter, kids, fifteen cents a pound!" (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: It's supposed to be grace, right?

speaker: IMN
words: It's supposed to be grace, but that s what the youngster's

speaker: IMN
words: father- the first thing he said! (Chuckles) It's a little different

speaker: IMN
words: than now, the price.

speaker: SS
words: What about the debates? Do you remember any of the sort of thing

speaker: SS
words: that- you say serious- the sort of thing the people would talk about?

speaker: SS
words: Was it politics or religion?

speaker: IMN
words: Well, no, it'd be politics and different subjects- wish I could re-

speaker: IMN
words: member some of I don't. I know when I went to school and the

speaker: IMN
words: first debate I ever was in. I don't know what grade I was in, it

speaker: IMN
words: must have been about the sixth- fifth or sixth- and the topic was

speaker: IMN
words: "Resolved that having the printing press was much more important

speaker: IMN
words: than the discovery of America." And I was on the printing press side,

speaker: IMN
words: And my teacher was printing the paper, so of course, I thought I had

speaker: IMN
words: a real, real good point, so I says, "Well, if the printing press

speaker: IMN
words: hadn't of been founded, my teacher couldn't have printed the paper."

speaker: IMN
words: And when the opponent got up he says, "No, he couldn't have printed

speaker: IMN
words: this paper, but the paper isn't any good anyway!" So that was the

speaker: IMN
words: end of our debate!

speaker: SS
words: The end of all the debates?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, the teacher didn't let us have any more debates! (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: Because of that?

speaker: IMN
words: I don't know, I imagine so. If we did have, I don't remember.

speaker: SS
words: Did he put out the Cameron paper? Did he make any money, you think,

speaker: SS
words: doing that?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, not too much, sort of on the side.

speaker: SS
words: Was that paper in German at the time?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, no. That was english. That was when we were going to English

speaker: IMN
words: school.

speaker: SS
words: Was the paper for the whole community?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, whoever subscribed for it, just the same as any other paper.

speaker: IMN
words: It was just news from Cameron and surrounding territory Just a

speaker: IMN
words: little pamphlet.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I'm real curious about some of the customs when you were youngthat

speaker: SS
words: people still kept that were from the old country- the sort of

speaker: SS
words: things that have died out since then.

speaker: IMN
words: Well, the celebration of Christmas, and giving Christmas dinners and

speaker: IMN
words: family together at Christmas at one of the- I think was handed down.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a family or was it a community celebration?

speaker: IMN
words: Family. That was a family celebration. Bee, I was trying to think

speaker: IMN
words: of some community celebrations. We used to have picnics. I remember.

speaker: IMN
words: And then one of the biggest things was on Christmas Eve, to go to church

speaker: IMN
words: and the children would have new dresses and new clothes, and that was

speaker: IMN
words: on« of the big things in the community.

speaker: SS
words: You would go to church Christmas Eve? Was there a celebration at

speaker: SS
words: church besides the service?

speaker: IMN
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: That was all at home.

speaker: IMN
words: That was all- yes. We didn't have any celebrations then, we have nowmore

speaker: IMN
words: so now than we used to years ago.

speaker: SS
words: I know Frank Brocke and you probably know him-

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. I know him.

speaker: SS
words: He told me how his father used to always dress up as Santa Claus and

speaker: SS
words: come in and the kids were always scared of him, they were always sure

speaker: SS
words: he was Santa Claus even they knew he was their father, they couldn't

speaker: SS
words: recognize him.

speaker: IMN
words: we always got some gift for Christmas, but never Santa Clausbecause

speaker: IMN
words: Santa Claus would come in a sleigh, and of course, there was

speaker: IMN
words: always one little thing- but we didn't get very many things- people

speaker: IMN
words: just couldn't afford it. And, of course, the next morning, we'd

speaker: IMN
words: say, "Yeah, we heard Santa Claus." And one thing we always did, we

speaker: IMN
words: always decorated the tree; everyone did and they made a lot of their

speaker: IMN
words: decorations. Once in a while we make 'em now to show people

speaker: IMN
words: what kind of decorations we used to have. Then Easter- there's another

speaker: IMN
words: thing they'd celebrate, you know, Easter Day. We'd havewe

speaker: IMN
words: didn't have eggs all winter- saved up a few eggs, so we could have

speaker: IMN
words: some for Easter. And I was just trying to think. the Fourth of

speaker: IMN
words: July celebrations- was always celebrated.

speaker: SS
words: That would have to be an American one.

speaker: IMN
words: That's an American one. And I was just thinking what other holidays

speaker: IMN
words: there were- those I think vrere the traditional holidays.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard about there was low German and a High German in

speaker: SS
words: the dialect.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, and I can talk both of 'em. My dad and his mother talked Low

speaker: IMN
words: German and we children talked Low German to them, and my mother didn't

speaker: IMN
words: talk Low German, she talked the regular German-High German and we

speaker: IMN
words: talked High German to her.

speaker: SS
words: Now what was the difference? I mean,why were there two different

speaker: SS
words: ones? Depends on where you come from?

speaker: IMN
words: Well,no, they had different dialects in Germany the same as they have

speaker: IMN
words: here, if you're from the South or from the North or from the West

speaker: IMN
words: your dialects are a little bit different.

speaker: SS
words: Why did they call the one Low and the other one High?

speaker: IMN
words: I don't know why it was called Low- but the Low German is not a

speaker: IMN
words: German, the High German- the regular German is, German

speaker: IMN
words: and the Low German is not the German.

speaker: SS
words: The regular German is that what they use in the church?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a lot of difference between 'em?

speaker: IMN
words: Oh, yes, there's a lot of difference. I was just trying to think-

speaker: IMN
words: Maybe if you'd give me a word, maybe I could tell oou maybe I could

speaker: IMN
words: tell you whether it was in High German or Low German.

speaker: SS
words: Well, what about something like school?

speaker: IMN
words: School? Shull That would be in German- ?

speaker: SS
words: Or like church?

speaker: IMN
words: Church? (can't spell the German) And they didn't have it in the High German.

speaker: SS
words: Well, could people who could only speak one or the other, could they

speaker: SS
words: speak to each other easily and make themselves understood?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Yes I think they could pretty well understand one another.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there was much desire to become Americanized here?

speaker: IMN
words: Very, very much so...My Grandfather came from Germany- he became an

speaker: IMN
words: American citizen right away because he wanted to be an American citi

speaker: IMN
words: zen. And of course, then, the Civil War broke out and he was in the

speaker: IMN
words: Civil War in Sherman's March to the Sea. And my folks always taught

speaker: IMN
words: us to be very good Americans, that's what they came to this country

speaker: IMN
words: for. And that's why we had these Fourth of July celebrations- well

speaker: IMN
words: there was a lot, a lot of patriotism shown. Always had a speaker

speaker: IMN
words: to talk about the good of this country and different things. And

speaker: IMN
words: then they usually eneded up - the program- with singing America and

speaker: IMN
words: every one sang and really meant it.

speaker: SS
words: But, say like, from day-to-day though, since this was mostly all

speaker: SS
words: German community right here- so people probably didn't have too much

speaker: SS
words: contact with people that weren't German, at least when they were

speaker: SS
words: right here.

speaker: IMN
words: Not right at Cameron, but if they did their trading at Kendrick and

speaker: IMN
words: that was English- so it was just community right in itself

speaker: IMN
words: was German, but as far as trading with Lewiston and so on. And I

speaker: IMN
words: know in- when the First World War broke out they sold more War Bonds

speaker: IMN
words: they really supported their country.

speaker: SS
words: That's funny, because I know I've heard that some people did- Frank

speaker: SS
words: Brocke talked to me and other people, too, who were German and they

speaker: SS
words: have told me that people got a hard time because they were German

speaker: SS
words: during that time. Some people gave 'em a hard time.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, there were some people. That's one reason my mother never

speaker: IMN
words: liked to talk much when she was out. She spoke broken English,

speaker: IMN
words: She had the accent, and it always bothered her. And nowdays if she

speaker: IMN
words: would be talking now-

speaker: SS
words: But at that time, I've heard- I heard that especially like Leland

speaker: SS
words: there were people in Leland that really gave Cameron a hard time

speaker: SS
words: during that time.

speaker: IMN
words: I can tell you something now- something that happened after the

speaker: IMN
words: First World War and I'm not going to mention any names because I

speaker: IMN
words: wouldn't want any names being mentioned- someone tore down- the flag

speaker: IMN
words: schoolhouse waan't taken down for some reason or other at

speaker: IMN
words: night and somebody came there and took the flag and stomped it down

speaker: IMN
words: into the ground, to give the German people a bad name. Of course,

speaker: IMN
words: that person has died or moved away from here a long time ago, so I

speaker: IMN
words: hope that I don't hurt any feelings, I just loved the people.

speaker: IMN
words: And there was someone at the schoolhouse that night and saw this.

speaker: SS
words: Saw it done?

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, and they weren't supposed to be there so they couldn't say any

speaker: IMN
words: thing. Of course, I knew it at the time, but I never said anything

speaker: IMN
words: because I wasn't supposed to say anything, and I wasn't going to tell

speaker: IMN
words: and get someone into trouble. So those things did happen.

speaker: SS
words: Were they there on or something like that?

speaker: IMN
words: At night; that's it. They were there on date. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: I think it was Hattie Johnson that told me that- now, I don't know

speaker: SS
words: if you remember this happening, but she said that when boys were going

speaker: SS
words: off to war at one point, there was a minister from Kendrick that

speaker: SS
words: really gave them a hard time. You remember that?

speaker: IMN
words: No, I don't, although her brother went at the same time my brothers

speaker: IMN
words: went. Now, it could be.

speaker: SS
words: She said, I think she said that he gave a farewell dinner and he said

speaker: SS
words: some things like, you know- "If I had any German blood, I would get

speaker: SS
words: rid of it." You know. Real patriotic.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes. Now, we were always taught, ray mother always taught us that

speaker: IMN
words: it doesn't make any difference what color you are, or what creed, or

speaker: IMN
words: what nationality, or how much education you have or how little edu

speaker: IMN
words: cation you have, it doesn't make any difference, it's what you do

speaker: IMN
words: with yourself and do with the rest of the people. That's what counts.

speaker: IMN
words: And so, we always tried to live by that.

speaker: SS
words: When I heard what I heard, it seemed very unfair.

speaker: IMN
words: Yes, there were incidents; I don't know of any incident right now,

speaker: IMN
words: but . do know even in World War II- now all of my children were inboys

speaker: IMN
words: were in- one of 'em was a German prisoner, he was shot down

speaker: IMN
words: over? and another one was in Patton's Army and one boy

speaker: IMN
words: was in Korea and the daughter took her nurse's training through the

speaker: IMN
words: services. They were all in it, I know that before the second boy

speaker: IMN
words: went, he was ahead of his age in school,,they were wondering why

speaker: IMN
words: he wasn't in the army and he was only seventeen years old. So, I

speaker: IMN
words: know that those things happen.

speaker: SS
